does: This paper proposes a unified framework for evaluating interpretability methods through their ability to control model behavior via intervention. The authors integrate four popular interpretability techniques—sparse autoencoders, logit lens, tuned lens, and probing—into a common encoder-decoder architecture that maps model latent representations to interpretable features and back. They introduce success criteria for assessing how effectively these methods enable intervention-based control of language model outputs, moving beyond traditional interpretability evaluation metrics that focus purely on explanation quality. The framework is applied to evaluate these methods' practical utility in actually steering model behavior rather than merely understanding internal representations.
connects: This work bridges two historically separate research directions: mechanistic interpretability (explaining how models work) and model steering/control (aligning and directing model behavior). It builds on foundational work in sparse autoencoders and feature-based interpretability, extending recent visualization methods like logit lens and tuned lens that reveal how information flows through transformer layers. The paper responds to growing recognition that understanding alone is insufficient without actionable control, drawing on broader alignment research that emphasizes the need for both interpretability and steering. It addresses a key limitation in the interpretability field: the lack of standardized evaluation metrics and unified frameworks for comparing diverse methods across consistent benchmarks.
enables: This unification framework enables more systematic evaluation and improvement of interpretability-for-control methods, potentially accelerating development of reliable steering techniques for increasingly capable language models. By establishing clear success criteria tied to intervention efficacy, the work supports better assessment of which interpretability approaches actually translate to practical control capabilities. The abstract encoder-decoder framework provides a foundation for designing hybrid methods that optimize jointly for both interpretability and controllability rather than treating them separately. Future work can build standardized evaluation suites, develop new methods tailored to intervention success, and extend these techniques to more complex control tasks, supporting the broader goal of AI alignment through mechanistic understanding and targeted behavior modification.
